title: Chapter 28
text: Chapter Twenty - Five A conversation drifted into Lara’s awareness as she woke, though the words were distant and fuzzy . “…electromagnetic pulse that damaged the circuitry of most electronics, robots included.” That was Newton’s voice, using more terms she didn’t understand. For all she knew, he was speaking a different language . She opened her eyes. The blank concrete wall loomed before her. As though sensing her wakefulness, Ronin brushed a hand down her arm . “This happened everywhere?” he asked . How long had he and Newton been talking? Lara had listened for a long time, asking questions when she couldn’t resist. Ronin was even older than he’d thought. He had existed in that long-ago, dead world, and she couldn’t help but wonder — had he cared for someone then like he did for Lara now ? Even through another Black Out, I could never forget you, Lara Brooks . But what if he’d forgotten someone else ? Exhaustion eventually caught up with her — helped along by the constant use of words she didn’t know or understand — and she had laid down on one of the bunks, drifting into a quick sleep while their voices continued behind her . “We had to assume so. There was no question that Denver suffered a direct strike. Fortunately — not that I can easily state anything was fortunate about the situation — the wind carried the fallout to the south…an oddity, considering that the prevailing wind typically blew from the south, at that time. Air current patterns have altered radically since then, of course, but—my apologies. I’ve a tendency to talk around my point . “There were no further communications, after Denver was destroyed .” “You said we .” Ronin glanced at Lara as she sat up and scooted beside him. He took her hand in his and turned his attention back to Newton . “I was assigned to the Air Force base in Cheyenne, working with William and his son during the war. Though portions of the city — specifically those in the vicinity of the freeway — were destroyed, there are heavily fortified facilities beneath the surface that weathered the worst of it. When I left, there were one hundred and nine personnel, civilian and military, surviving .” Lara’s brow creased. “What do you mean, beneath Cheyenne ?” Newton looked at her. His appearance didn’t seem so strange after seeing Ronin without skin. “Not beneath Cheyenne, Miss Brooks. Beneath Francis E. Warren Air Force Base, west of the city proper .” She raked her fingers through her tangled hair, pulling it out of her face. “There’s people there? Still ?” “It’s been many years since I was last there. Though they were, perhaps, limited on space, they were thriving .” There were people thriving , so close to Cheyenne? That meant they had food, secure shelter… She turned to Ronin. “Warlord doesn’t know they’re there !” “Warlord,” Newton echoed, curling one of his hands over the other. “I sometimes forget that’s what he calls himself. He suspected they were there, at the very least. Took his followers and scoured the entire surface of the base, searching for weapons and material, though there wasn’t much left after the war. They dismantled whatever they couldn’t take. Fortunately, his impatience won out long before he found a way down .” “You know him?” Ronin’s forehead creased . “As much as I would prefer to deny it, yes. I repaired and reactivated him. For a time, it seemed as though it would be to the benefit of many. He had such…vision. It is an uncommon trait for robots to possess so strongly. But…he was never like the rest of us .” “What do you mean?” Lara asked. “You mean he was always against humans ?” “Not at all , Miss — ” “Just Lara,” she said, cringing inwardly . “Lara, then. He had no more issue with humans than the rest of us, at least initially. Whatever information his data core retained through his deactivation must simply have left him with no conclusions to draw save that humans were responsible for what happened. Therefore, he does not believe them worthy of his trust or goodwill .” “Are there others like him out there ?” “I’ve never seen one like him,” Ronin said. “Bots and humans live separate in many places. Sometimes, with tension between them. But I’ve never been anyplace else where bots slaughtered humans, the way he has .” How many people had Warlord killed? Not just when the man in the attic had been alive, but in all the years after ? “To my knowledge,” Newton’s fingers fidgeted, “he is the only unit of his kind. He has a…unique means of processing that was still in an experimental phase during the war. Given more time, there would certainly have been more of his ilk. I think it is for the best, perhaps, that such research was ended .” “Experimental phase?” Lara drew her knees up and wrapped her arms around her legs. “Like how they changed Ronin ?” “There was nothing experimental in what was done to Ronin .” Ronin put an arm around Lara’s shoulders, and she leaned into him. Since the first time they made love, he’d been unable to keep his hands off her — as though he craved the physical contact as much as she did . “What, exactly, was done to me?” Ronin asked . “As I said, you were originally built to mimic a human. To be able to pass as one — or at least closely enough that the differences would be of little concern. When war erupted, and the scale of it increased, the supply of robots constructed specifically for military use was exhausted rapidly . “The governments of the world seized all the civilian units they could and repurposed them. You were one amongst hundreds of thousands. Your neural interface was upgraded to allow for increased reaction time, and they installed advanced optics and targeting systems. Your strength and speed inhibitors were disabled, allowing use of your full capabilities. And your casing was reinforced for increased durability. Synthetic humans were especially desirable for military applications, as the psychological effects of being unable to distinguish man from machine upon human combatants were significant over long periods of time .” “He was turned into a soldier,” Lara said . “Robots do not tire,” Newton explained. “We can react with greater speed and accuracy than humans are capable of. We are stronger, faster, and more durable, and whatever damage we may suffer can be repaired in a relatively short amount of time. And, at the time, we were not considered by most to be living beings. We were weapons, expensive but expendable. Robots in combat meant fewer human lives put at risk .” “You just told us humans and bots coexisted peacefully back then. Why would they make us fight their war?” Ronin asked . Newton shook his head, lowering his gaze. His fingers shifted again in an almost nervous gesture. It reminded her of Ronin occasionally scratching his cheek when he seemed particularly conflicted or irritated . “Nothing was ever quite that simple. The politics and power balances in the word were woven in a complicated web. Many humans viewed robots as tools, more akin to the handheld electronic devices they were so infatuated with than to themselves. Many others, like William, recognized our emergent consciousness, intelligence, and personality, and saw something inherently human in mind, if not in body . “Fear played a large part in all of it, of course. We were feared for what we were. Superior to humans in so many ways — and allow me to apologize, Lara, as I intend no offense in that statement .” “It’s nothing I don’t already know,” she said, glancing up at Ronin. She’d feared him initially , too . “William and his colleagues recognized that robots and humans could be of immense benefit to one another, so long as the relationship was approached with good faith and open-mindedness. He saw it as a codependency that would help realize a new age of technology and convenience for all humanity. I believed it, too.” Newton’s hand’s parted, fingers stretching and freezing . “But there were those who feared for the future of humanity. It was a common sentiment, amongst those in opposition to our increased freewill, that we would eventually overtake humans, annihilate them, and claim the world as our own. That spark of fear ignited into hatred, and that hatred eventually helped fuel a war that consumed the entire world .” “They caused the thing they wanted to prevent,” Ronin said . Newton nodded, pressing his hands back together. “Not despite their efforts, but — in part — because of them .” “But that prejudice went both ways, didn’t it?” Lara asked. “Warlord did take over, at least in Cheyenne, just like they feared .” “Warlord…” Newton hung his head and trailed off, lapsing into silence . “You’re hiding something.” Lara narrowed her eyes on him. Ronin’s fingers tightened on her shoulder. “What makes Warlord so different ?” “He’s harmed countless people,” Ronin added, “and bots, too. Anyone who opposes him .” Newton stared down at his hands, intertwining his fingers, brushing his thumbs together. Just as Lara was about to demand an answer, he spoke . “His name was Kevin Turner. He was a human .” Lara’s breath fled her lungs. Her eyes widened, and she couldn’t form any words, couldn’t form a coherent thought . “Kevin Turner was diagnosed with inoperable, terminal cancer when he was forty-three years old, given an estimated three months to live. Doctor Anderson was, at the time, one of the foremost scientists in the world working on the next frontier in robotics — the transfer of human consciousness into a robotic body .” “I don’t understand how that would be possible,” Ronin said. “Everything about the way we think and function is completely different .” “Not so different as it would appear. We both feel and react through a neural interface that sends electric signals from sensors — or nerve endings — to a central control point. The coding that guides our thought and behavior is, in some ways, similar to the coding of the human brain. It was just another challenge to overcome, and William never shied away from challenges. Additionally, he was working with Doctor Jessica Yuan, one of the most talented neurologists in her field .” “Why?” Lara forced herself to ask . “Why?” Newton repeated thoughtfully. “For William, it was a way to provide families more time with their loved ones. I believe Doctor Yuan felt the same way. What medical science could not overcome alone, perhaps robotics could conquer. Kevin Turner had a wife and two children who would have been without a husband and father when he passed away. He volunteered for the program after Doctor Yuan explained the possibilities — and the risks .” “I-If he was human, why does he hate us ?” “I wish I could offer you a definitive answer, Lara. All I can do is speculate. The transfer was successful, but not without complications. The stresses he endured were massive. The psychological ramifications were only just being explored when the military seized Kevin and all associated research. We were all transported to an undisclosed location, from which we were eventually transferred to Francis E. Warren Air Force Base .” “I didn’t see Kevin again, until I found him in the wasteland years later and reactivated him. There was no indication, during our brief interaction, that he recalled having been human .” “How bad was the war?” Lara asked, rubbing her tired eyes with the heels of her hands . “It was terrible,” Ronin replied. His grip on her tightened. “I see faces, sometimes. Glimpses into my damaged memories. All people and bots I’ve ended .” “So…you remember stuff from before the Black Out?” She glanced up at him, frowning . “Only flickers. Still images of death and deactivation .” “Could he ever regain the rest?” Lara swung her gaze back to Newton . “Though not impossible, it is improbable. They had the necessary equipment at the Air Force Base, but there is no guarantee that it is still operable, or that his data is not corrupted beyond recovery .” “I don’t think I want it back .” “Why?” Lara returned her attention to Ronin . “What is there for me, in those memories?” He raised his free hand to his cheek and scratched. “Pain, loss, the reminder of all the terrible things I did and witnessed? If there was anyone in my life before, they’re gone now .” Lara cringed, and he turned his head to her and cupped her face . “What I have with you, Lara, has brought me more contentment than anything else. The memories I am making with you render everything before useless to me .” “What if there was someone you…you felt the same for as me, but just forgot about?” She clenched her fists in her lap . “If there was anyone I ever felt the same about, I would never have forgotten. You are burned into every circuit, every bit of data. You are part of my core function now .” She covered his hands with her own and leaned her forehead against his, closing her eyes. How had she come to need him, in so short a time? Ronin had worked his way into every part of her being. When he’d pushed her away, distanced himself because of his own pain, she’d felt it in her soul. She needed him, needed his solid, unwavering presence, his patience, his thoughtfulness. She craved his touch not just in passion, but in comfort . Tabitha leaving home had been painful, and her death had left a hole in Lara’s heart that would never heal . But she wasn’t sure she’d survive losing Ronin . “We left Cheyenne so we could be together.” She turned her head to look at Newton. “My sister went to live with a bot. Warlord killed them both. He gave Ronin a chance to prove himself, by killing me .” “We never hypothesized that the transferal of his consciousness would be the catalyst that eliminated his humanity,” Newton said softly. “That was never our intention .” “Can he be stopped ?” Newton nodded. “The same as anyone else .” “Then why hasn’t he been?” she demanded, fire sparking in her belly. All the suffering, fear, and death. “You turned him on. Why didn’t you turn him off ?” “Were he still human, and I merely the physician who treated him after a terrible injury, would you ask that question of me ?” “ Yes !” His metallic frown deepened, and his body sagged. “I am not a destroyer. My goal was to assist the world’s recovery, but that aim has failed utterly. In Cheyenne, especially, my intervention has only heightened people’s suffering. That’s why I’ve been here. Perhaps, at one point, I believed I could do it. After hearing rumors of the things he’d done… I am not designed to destroy, Lara. So, I’ve laid here for years, hiding from the world I wanted to help .” “Why not get someone else to help, then? Someone to do the dirty work? No, you just sat here, rusting away while he killed people. Not just humans, but everyone!” She glared at him, chest heaving, but the fire snuffed out as realization dawned. “And we just left them , too .” Hanging her head, she stabbed her fingers into her hair, tugging it back . Ronin wrapped his arms around her and pulled her into his embrace. His hold was gentle, but firm. “You know that’s not how we work. Not anymore than it is how you do .” “Bad humans get punished. Why not bots , too ?” “He has a small army around himself. Who could oppose that?” Ronin asked . “Other bots, like you.” She lifted her head to meet his eyes . “I’m only one. Even if I managed to walk up and deactivate him permanently, I’d never get out in one piece .” Lara flung her hand toward Newton. “He’s the fucking Prophet ! You recognized him. Wouldn’t others? There have to be other bots in Cheyenne who would oppose Warlord, and they’d trust Newton .” “The Prophet is a legend, Lara. I’m just a bot designed to assist in scientific research and experimentation .” “But they’d listen to you. You know things. You turned a lot of them on, didn’t you ?” Newton raised his hands, turning them to look at his scratched palms. “I turned them on, yes, but it was individuals like Warlord who provided them the things they required to function properly .” “ Ronin provides the things they need. Him and the other dustwalkers bring in the goods .” “That’s true,” Ronin said, “but it doesn’t matter. I’m still an outsider in Cheyenne, existing apart from the community he built .” “Built? Humans already built it, he just took it over!” Lara pulled away from him. How could she not feel guilty for leaving now, having learned all this? She hadn’t even said a word to Gary and Kate, who’d helped her despite having no obligation to do so, whose lives hung by a thread that Warlord could cut at any moment. “He’ll kill them all. Every single one of them. It’s just a matter of time. And you’d just sit here and do nothing . ” “What would you have me do?” Newton asked, meeting her gaze. “Are the three of us to walk into the center of town and attempt to rally robots to our cause? You know what he’s capable of, Lara. You’ve seen it firsthand .” “The humans would fight. We’ve got nothing else to lose. And what about the people in the base? If you told them what he’s doing, would they just sit back and wait until he eventually finds them ?” “We can’t go back to Cheyenne.” There was steel in Ronin’s voice. “It’s not safe for you .” “Is anywhere safe?” Lara closed her eyes and inhaled. Her throat was tight, and she was on the verge of tears. She was demanding a lot, pushing against things well beyond her control. Even if she hadn’t interacted with most of Cheyenne’s humans, they were still her people, and they’d all been forced into lives of fear by Warlord. She’d grown up around them, knew their faces, had traded with them. She’d recognized the suffering in the eyes of the children running through the filthy streets. It was the same suffering she’d known her whole life . And all she could do was run away. She hated feeling so helpless. Hated that there was nothing they could do. It was wrong, but what choice did they have ? “You’re right,” she whispered. “We can’t go back .” “It may be difficult to believe,” Ronin said, placing a warm hand under her chin and guiding her eyes to his, “but there is an entire world beyond Cheyenne. We can make a future somewhere, and this may be the only chance we have to do so together .” Lara nodded. However terrible the situation was in Cheyenne, she had something to live for. Someone . Mental exhaustion tumbled atop her, weighing her down; this was too much, too quickly. She sagged into Ronin’s embrace, resting her head on his shoulder . Newton said something quietly, but she’d already closed her eyes and stopped paying attention . Once the storm was over and they left this place, she’d see the world, and she was glad Ronin would be the one to show her. However, there would always be a patch of gloom overshadowing their happiness, knowing she’d done nothing to help the others .
